name: Agent Interoperability Protocols — MCP, A2A, ACP, ANP (2025)
origin_domain: AI industry (multi-agent communication standards, protocol engineering, web standards)
year_developed: 2024-2025 (MCP: Anthropic, November 2024; A2A: Google, April 2025; ACP: IBM / Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, 2025; ANP: open-source community, China-origin, 2025)
progenitor: MCP: Anthropic (AI research lab); A2A: Google (DeepMind / Google Cloud); ACP: IBM Research and Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications (BUPT), contributed to Linux Foundation; ANP: open-source community project initiated by Chinese AI developers, Apache 2.0 licensed
driving_insight: The agent ecosystem in 2025 faces a fundamental interoperability crisis: AI agents built on different platforms (Anthropic, Google, open-source) cannot communicate with each other or share tools. This fragments the agent landscape into isolated silos — an Anthropic Claude-powered agent cannot delegate a task to a Google Gemini-powered agent, and neither can discover or use tools registered in a third-party registry. Four competing protocols have emerged to solve this, each with a different philosophy: MCP treats the LLM as the center of the universe (tools and data connect to the model), A2A treats agents as peers in an enterprise team, ACP treats agents as structured message exchangers, and ANP treats agents as autonomous participants in a decentralized network. The key insight is that NO SINGLE PROTOCOL WILL 'WIN' — they serve different layers (capability, collaboration, network) and will coexist, requiring agent gateways for cross-protocol interoperability. The IEEE has begun standardization efforts, and a 2025 survey paper (arXiv:2505.02279) provides the first comprehensive comparison.
decision_style: Protocol-design-driven — each protocol specifies decision points for discovery, authentication, message routing, and session management. At the protocol level, decisions are algorithmic (JSON-RPC for MCP, RESTful for ACP, DAG of Task/Artifact for A2A, DID-based for ANP). At the agent level, the protocols are neutral — they do not prescribe how agents make decisions, only how they communicate their decisions to each other. The decision style for the protocols themselves is: MCP = centralized (client-server with tools registered to a host), A2A = semi-centralized (agent cards registered at well-known URIs), ACP = client-server RESTful (structured message contracts), ANP = decentralized (peer-to-peer with DID identity).
problem_class_targeted: All protocols target the same meta-problem: enabling heterogeneous AI agents to communicate, share tools, delegate tasks, and coordinate in multi-agent workflows. The specific sub-problems vary: MCP targets tool-use and data access for single-LLM-to-multiple-tools scenarios (the 'AI's USB-C port'); A2A targets enterprise multi-agent collaboration (agents delegating sub-tasks to other agents); ACP targets structured, semantically rich multi-agent messaging with strong identity and contract enforcement; ANP targets open, decentralized agent networks where agents from different organizations discover and transact with each other without central authority.
ordered_phases: Protocol operation phases (common across protocols with different implementations): (1) Discovery — Agents register their capabilities (MCP: manual/static tool registration; A2A: Agent Card at /.well-known/agent.json; ACP: Agent Manifest at /.well-known/agent.yml; ANP: W3C DID documents on distributed ledger). (2) Authentication — Agents prove their identity (MCP: OAuth 2.0 / API Key; A2A: OAuth 2.0 / IAM / JWS; ACP: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE / JWT / mTLS; ANP: W3C DID + cryptographic signatures with optional zero-knowledge proofs). (3) Capability Negotiation — Agents exchange capability descriptions to determine compatibility (MCP: tool list via JSON-RPC; A2A: Agent Card includes task types; ACP: Agent Manifest declares message schemas; ANP: Schema.org + JSON-LD semantic descriptions). (4) Task/Messaging — Agents exchange structured messages (MCP: JSON-RPC 2.0 tool calls; A2A: Task/Artifact JSON messages; ACP: MIME multipart + JSON metadata; ANP: JSON-LD messages over P2P transport). (5) Session Management — State tracking across multi-step interactions (MCP: optional; A2A: native task/session model; ACP: multi-round session with state persistence; ANP: meta-protocol negotiation for session semantics). (6) Termination — Sessions/tasks close (MCP: connection close; A2A: task completion/artifact delivery; ACP: session end with state archiving; ANP: connection teardown).
feedback_loops: Protocol-level feedback loops: (1) Discovery refresh — agent capabilities may change, requiring periodic re-discovery (A2A, ANP support dynamic capability updates; MCP requires manual re-registration); (2) Error feedback — all protocols define error message formats (JSON-RPC error codes for MCP, structured error messages for A2A/ACP/ANP); (3) Capability degradation — if an agent fails to respond, discovery mechanisms can mark it as unavailable (native in A2A/ACP/ANP, not in MCP). At the architecture level: the 'layered coexistence' model (MCP for capability, A2A/ACP for collaboration, ANP for networking) is an implicit feedback loop — each layer compensates for gaps in the others.
gates: Protocol-level gates: (1) Authentication gate — unauthenticated requests are rejected (all protocols); (2) Authorization gate — authenticated agents may lack permission for specific operations (A2A, ACP, ANP have fine-grained authorization; MCP has tool-level permissions); (3) Schema validation gate — malformed messages are rejected (ACP has strict MIME multipart validation; MCP validates JSON-RPC structure; ANP validates JSON-LD semantics); (4) Rate limiting gate — all protocols recommend rate limiting to prevent abuse. These gates are enforcement points in the protocol implementation, not decision gates in the planning sense.
exit_criteria: Protocol-level: a task/message exchange is complete when the final response (artifact, result, acknowledgment) is delivered and the session is closed. For long-running agent tasks: the protocol itself does not define task completion — this is delegated to the agent implementation. MCP: a tool call returns a result. A2A: a task transitions to completed state and artifacts are delivered. ACP: a message exchange reaches terminal state. ANP: the meta-protocol negotiates completion semantics per interaction.
decomposition_method: Not a decomposition method — the protocols define HOW agents communicate, not HOW they decompose problems. However, the choice of protocol influences decomposition strategy: MCP encourages tool-centric decomposition (break tasks into tool calls); A2A encourages role-centric decomposition (break tasks into agent responsibilities); ACP encourages message-centric decomposition (break interactions into structured message exchanges); ANP encourages capability-based decomposition (break tasks into services offered by autonomous agents). The survey paper notes that protocol choice is downstream of decomposition strategy, not a substitute for it.
granularity_guidance: Protocol-level granularity: MCP operates at the tool-call level (fine-grained); A2A operates at the task/sub-task level (medium-grained); ACP operates at the message exchange level (medium-grained); ANP operates at the agent capability level (coarse-grained to fine-grained, negotiated per interaction). The protocol choice constrains granularity: MCP is too fine-grained for agent-to-agent delegation (every agent call looks like a tool call); A2A is too structured for ad-hoc peer discovery (requires pre-known agent cards); ANP's decentralization adds overhead that may be unnecessary for tightly coupled agent teams.
boundary_rules: Protocols define boundary rules at the communication layer: (1) Message contract boundaries — MCP uses JSON-RPC method signatures; A2A uses Task/Artifact schemas; ACP uses MIME multipart message types; ANP uses JSON-LD semantic types; (2) Trust boundaries — MCP trusts the host; A2A trusts the IAM system; ACP trusts the OAuth 2.1 authorization server; ANP trusts the DID ledger; (3) State boundaries — MCP is stateless by default; A2A maintains session state; ACP maintains structured state; ANP leaves state to the meta-protocol. Clean boundaries are essential for cross-protocol gateways: a gateway must translate message formats, trust models, and state semantics between protocols.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Protocol-level risk mechanisms: (1) MCP: no built-in risk assessment — tools are trusted once registered; risk from malicious MCP servers prompted the 'MCP server poisoning' attack class (OWASP ASI04); (2) A2A: IAM-based risk assessment — agent identity and permissions are managed through enterprise identity systems; risk is assessed at the corporate security level; (3) ACP: structured message validation provides risk signals — malformed or unexpected message patterns can be flagged; (4) ANP: DID reputation systems can provide risk scores for agents based on interaction history; the decentralized model allows for trustless interactions with cryptographic verification. None of the protocols provide LLM-level risk assessment (evaluating the QUALITY of an agent's output) — this is left to the application layer.
unknown_handling: Protocol-level unknown handling: (1) MCP: unknown tool calls return JSON-RPC error (-32601 Method not found); (2) A2A: unknown task types are rejected by the receiving agent; (3) ACP: unknown message types trigger the error handling path defined in the message contract; (4) ANP: unknown capability requests can be routed through the discovery mechanism to find alternative agents. All protocols handle unknowns at the protocol level (message format, method name) but NOT at the semantic level (is this agent's output correct?). ANP's decentralized discovery is the most robust for unknown handling — if one agent cannot handle a request, the network can route to alternatives.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE — all four protocols reject what they cannot validate (unknown message types, unauthenticated requests, unauthorized operations). This is standard protocol design: be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept (Postel's Law). ANP's decentralized model adds a discovery-based escape hatch: unknowns can be routed to the network for resolution, but the default is still to reject locally until resolved.
safety_guardrails: Protocol-level safety: (1) Agent identity verification — all protocols require authentication before accepting messages, preventing impersonation; (2) Message validation — structured schemas prevent injection attacks at the protocol level (MCP JSON-RPC, A2A defined task schemas, ACP MIME multipart validation, ANP JSON-LD semantic validation); (3) Authorization — fine-grained permissions control what authenticated agents can do. Significant gaps: (1) NONE of the protocols address Lies-in-the-Loop attacks — the protocols authenticate the agent but do not verify the TRUTHFULNESS of its messages; (2) Prompt injection is not addressed — an agent receiving a message from an authenticated peer may still be vulnerable to injection if the peer is compromised; (3) Tool misuse is out of scope — the protocols enable agent-to-tool communication but do not restrict what tools can be called; (4) MCP's static tool registration creates a supply-chain risk — compromised MCP servers can poison all connected agents (OWASP ASI04).
human_decision_points: Protocol-level: humans are involved in: (1) Protocol selection — choosing which protocol(s) to implement for a given agent system; (2) Authentication/authorization policy — defining identity and permission rules; (3) Agent registration — approving agent cards/manifests; (4) Gateway configuration — setting up cross-protocol translation rules. At runtime, none of the protocols mandate human-in-the-loop approval of agent communications (though A2A supports it as an implementation choice). The human is OFF the protocol communication loop but ON the protocol governance loop.
override_capability: Protocol-level: humans can override at the governance level (revoke agent credentials, update authentication policies, block specific agent communications). At runtime, overrides depend on the implementation: A2A supports task cancellation (a human can cancel an in-progress task); MCP supports tool unregistration. The granularity is typically at the agent/task level (block this agent, cancel this task) rather than per-message.
fallback_when_no_human: Protocols operate normally without humans — they are designed for automated agent-to-agent and agent-to-tool communication. If an agent's credentials are revoked (human action during an emergency), the protocol rejects further communication from that agent. If no human is available to resolve a blocked state, the protocol times out — default behavior is to reject rather than auto-approve.
max_problem_size: Protocol scalability: MCP supports a single LLM host with hundreds of registered tools (practical ceiling: context window limits how many tool descriptions can be listed per call); A2A supports enterprise-scale agent fleets (tested at Google scale); ACP supports structured multi-agent networks with message contracts; ANP is designed for internet-scale decentralized agent networks with DHT-based discovery. What breaks at scale: MCP's centralized client-server model creates a bottleneck at the host; A2A's well-known URI discovery requires DNS for each agent domain; ACP's structured message contracts become unwieldy with many message types; ANP's DHT lookup latency increases with network size. The survey paper notes that protocol interoperability (agents communicating across protocol boundaries) is the primary scaling challenge — each cross-protocol gateway adds latency and complexity.
parallel_execution: Protocol-level parallelism: MCP supports concurrent tool calls from a single host; A2A supports parallel task delegation across multiple agents; ACP supports concurrent message exchanges; ANP supports parallel P2P interactions. Parallelism is determined by the protocol's concurrency model: MCP uses async JSON-RPC for non-blocking tool calls; A2A uses task DAGs with parallelizable branches; ACP uses async request-response; ANP uses concurrent P2P streams. Dependency resolution for parallel execution is out of protocol scope — it is handled by the agent orchestration layer (e.g., LangGraph, CrewAI).
team_scaling_model: Protocol scaling with team size: MCP (centralized client-server): linear for tool count, but the LLM host becomes a bottleneck; A2A (semi-centralized): sub-linear communication because agents only communicate through task delegation (not all-to-all), scaling is roughly O(n) for n agents; ACP (client-server RESTful): linear communication cost; ANP (decentralized P2P): O(log n) for discovery (DHT-based), O(k) for communication (where k is the number of direct peers). The survey paper notes that protocol choice significantly impacts team scaling: centralized protocols (MCP) degrade beyond dozens of tools; semi-centralized (A2A) works for hundreds of agents; decentralized (ANP) scales to thousands but with higher latency per interaction.
cost_scaling_model: Protocol overhead varies: MCP has the lowest per-message overhead (JSON-RPC is lightweight) but the centralized model concentrates cost on the host; A2A has moderate overhead (Agent Card discovery, session management) but efficient task delegation; ACP has higher per-message overhead (MIME multipart parsing) but richer semantics; ANP has the highest per-interaction overhead (DID resolution, cryptographic verification, DHT lookups) but enables trustless interactions. Protocol selection significantly impacts total agent system cost: using MCP for agent-to-agent communication forces every interaction through the tool-call abstraction, which may multiply calls; using ANP for tightly coupled agents adds unnecessary cryptographic overhead. The survey paper recommends: MCP for internal tool use, A2A/ACP for team collaboration, ANP for cross-organizational agent interactions.
replanning_built_in: Not applicable at the protocol level — protocols define communication rules, not planning strategies. However, protocol features affect replanning: A2A's task lifecycle model (including cancellation and re-delegation) supports replanning better than MCP's request-response model; ACP's structured state persistence enables replanning from saved checkpoints; ANP's decentralized discovery enables finding alternative agents when an initial delegate fails. The protocol choice constrains but does not determine the replanning capability of the agent system.
trigger_mechanism: Protocol-level triggers: communication failure (timeout, connection refused, authentication failure) triggers error handling defined by the protocol. These are protocol-level triggers, not planning-level triggers. A2A's task lifecycle includes explicit failure states that trigger error handling; MCP's JSON-RPC error codes trigger client-side error handling. ANP's discovery mechanism can trigger re-routing if the initial agent is unavailable.
adaptation_cost: Protocol adaptation cost: switching protocols requires re-implementing the agent's communication layer. The survey paper identifies 'protocol lock-in' as a significant risk — agents built deeply on MCP's tool semantics cannot easily switch to A2A's task semantics. Cross-protocol gateways reduce this cost but add latency and reduce semantic fidelity. The IEEE standardization effort (Agentic AI Protocol Working Group, launched late 2025) aims to reduce adaptation cost by defining common abstractions. The 'layered coexistence' model (MCP for capability, A2A/ACP for collaboration, ANP for networking) provides a migration path: adopt protocols incrementally by layer rather than switching wholesale.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Protocol-level failure recovery: (1) MCP: connection retry, re-registration; (2) A2A: task retry, re-delegation to alternative agent; (3) ACP: session recovery from persisted state; (4) ANP: re-discovery and re-routing to alternative peers. These mechanisms handle communication failures (network, authentication, timeout), not agent reasoning failures (the agent produced a wrong answer). For agent reasoning failures, the recovery mechanism is out of protocol scope and must be implemented by the agent orchestration layer. The cross-protocol survey paper identifies a gap: no protocol defines standard semantics for 'this agent's output was wrong, try again or try another agent.'
output_artifacts: Protocol-specified outputs: MCP produces tool call results (structured JSON); A2A produces Tasks (with status and metadata) and Artifacts (the actual output of agent work); ACP produces message exchanges with MIME multipart bodies; ANP produces JSON-LD documents with semantic annotations. The output format varies significantly across protocols, making cross-protocol translation a non-trivial mapping problem. The survey paper catalogs these format differences as a primary barrier to interoperability.
briefing_format: Protocol-level briefing: MCP provides tool lists and call logs; A2A provides task status dashboards (which tasks completed, which are pending, failure rates); ACP provides message exchange logs with timestamps and message types; ANP provides agent reputation scores and interaction histories. For human oversight, A2A has the richest briefing format (task-centric with clear statuses), followed by ACP (message-centric with structured logs), MCP (tool-call-centric, low-level), and ANP (decentralized, requires aggregation).
traceability: Protocol-level traceability: MCP provides tool-call-level traceability (which tool was called, with what parameters, returning what result); A2A provides task-level traceability (which agent performed which task, with what inputs/outputs, how long it took); ACP provides message-level traceability with cryptographic signing for non-repudiation; ANP provides DID-based provenance (which agent identity performed which action, verifiable via cryptographic signatures). ACP and ANP have the strongest traceability guarantees (cryptographic non-repudiation); MCP has the weakest (tools are trusted, no cryptographic verification of tool identity or output). For JORDAN's traceability requirements, A2A or ACP would provide the richest audit trails.
agent_communication_protocol: This item IS the analysis of agent communication protocols. The four protocols compared: (1) MCP (Anthropic) — JSON-RPC 2.0, centralized client-server, OAuth 2.0 / API Key auth, manual/static discovery; (2) A2A (Google) — custom JSON Task/Artifact messages, semi-centralized peer discovery via Agent Cards at /.well-known/agent.json, OAuth 2.0 / IAM / JWS auth; (3) ACP (IBM/BUPT, Linux Foundation) — MIME multipart + JSON metadata, client-server RESTful, OAuth 2.1 + PKCE / JWT / mTLS auth, Agent Manifest at /.well-known/agent.yml; (4) ANP (open-source community, Apache 2.0) — JSON-LD + Schema.org, decentralized P2P, W3C DID + cryptographic signatures with optional zero-knowledge proofs, DHT-based discovery. No single protocol dominates — the emerging consensus (arXiv:2505.02279) is a layered architecture: MCP for capability (tools), A2A/ACP for collaboration (messaging), ANP for networking (discovery).
learning_memory_architecture: Protocol-level: MCP has no built-in learning — tool registrations are static; A2A can record task histories for agent performance tracking; ACP supports session archiving for post-hoc analysis; ANP supports decentralized reputation systems that learn agent trustworthiness from interaction history. At the protocol selection level: organizations should build an evidence base (which protocol works best for which agent interaction patterns) rather than committing to one protocol. This meta-learning (protocol effectiveness data) is the most impactful form of memory for multi-agent system architects.
latency_bounds: Protocol latency characteristics: MCP has the lowest latency (JSON-RPC is minimal overhead, connection is persistent); A2A has moderate latency (Agent Card discovery adds an initial round-trip, task state management adds overhead per interaction); ACP has moderate-to-high latency (MIME multipart parsing, structured validation, session state persistence); ANP has the highest latency (DHT lookups for discovery, cryptographic verification, P2P connection establishment). For interactive agent systems: MCP or A2A are preferable. For batch/deferred agent interactions: ACP or ANP are acceptable. The cross-protocol gateway adds additional latency (message translation, trust model bridging).
evaluation_benchmarking: No standardized benchmarks for comparing agent communication protocols exist as of 2025. The survey paper (arXiv:2505.02279) provides qualitative comparison across 30+ dimensions but no quantitative benchmarks. Protocol evaluation criteria are qualitative: discovery mechanism robustness, authentication strength, message expressiveness, coupling degree, vendor lock-in risk, scalability ceiling, and ecosystem maturity. The IEEE Agentic AI Protocol Working Group (launched late 2025) is developing conformance test suites. Protocol choice is currently evaluated through architectural analysis rather than empirical benchmarking.
mapping_strength: Critical mapping to JORDAN: the protocol choice directly determines JORDAN's ability to interoperate with external agents and tools. Recommendation: use MCP for JORDAN agents' tool integration (web search, code execution, file I/O accessed through MCP servers); use A2A or ACP for inter-agent communication within JORDAN (plan node delegating to research node, research node returning to synthesize node); consider ANP for cross-project or cross-organization agent interactions. The protocol layer MUST be abstracted behind a unified agent communication interface so JORDAN can switch protocols or use multiple protocols simultaneously. The survey paper's layered model (MCP for capability, A2A/ACP for collaboration, ANP for networking) provides a template for JORDAN's multi-protocol architecture.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) No single protocol covers all communication patterns — JORDAN needs a multi-protocol strategy; (2) Protocol immaturity — all four protocols are from 2024-2025 and evolving rapidly, with breaking changes likely; (3) No standardized semantic layer — protocols handle syntax (message format) but not semantics (what does this message MEAN for the task?); (4) Cross-protocol translation is lossy — a task represented in A2A cannot be perfectly translated to MCP tool calls; (5) No protocol addresses Lies-in-the-Loop attacks — JORDAN's HITL dialogs are vulnerable to the same input manipulation that Checkmarx demonstrated; (6) Vendor lock-in risk — MCP (Anthropic) and A2A (Google) are controlled by single companies, creating dependency risk; (7) Protocol selection frameworks are qualitative — no empirical data guides which protocol to use for which agent interaction type; (8) Identity and trust models are incompatible across protocols — an agent authenticated via A2A's IAM cannot directly interact with an ANP agent using DID.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Implement a protocol abstraction layer — a unified AgentCommunicationInterface that supports MCP (for tools), A2A (for agent delegation), and ACP (for structured messaging) behind a single API; (2) Define a JORDAN-internal message schema that maps to all protocols — this is the canonical IR for inter-agent communication; (3) Implement protocol gateways/adapters that translate JORDAN's internal schema to each protocol's format; (4) Build a discovery registry for JORDAN agents — whether agents register via A2A Agent Cards or ACP Agent Manifests, JORDAN maintains a unified agent directory; (5) Design for protocol evolution — use the adapter pattern so that protocol changes affect only the adapter, not the agent logic; (6) Add Lies-in-the-Loop defenses — implement Markdown sanitization, length limits on HITL dialogs, and structured approval formats that resist input manipulation; (7) Contribute JORDAN's protocol usage data to the community to help build the empirical evidence base for protocol selection; (8) Monitor IEEE/W3C standardization progress and align JORDAN's protocol abstraction with emerging standards.
validation_context: Validated through: (1) Production deployment: MCP powers Anthropic's Claude Code and is integrated into LangChain, LangGraph, and hundreds of tools; A2A is deployed within Google's internal agent systems and select enterprise partners; ACP is contributed to Linux Foundation with reference implementations; ANP has community implementations and testnets. (2) Academic survey: arXiv:2505.02279 (May 2025) provides the first comprehensive 4-protocol comparison across 30+ dimensions. (3) Industry adoption: MCP has the widest ecosystem (2025: 1000+ MCP servers); A2A has Google-scale testing; ACP and ANP are earlier in adoption. The protocols are validated in the pragmatic sense (they work at scale for their intended use cases), not through formal protocol verification.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) MCP server poisoning — compromised MCP servers can inject malicious tool definitions, exploiting all connected LLMs (OWASP ASI04); (2) A2A centralization risk — Google controls the A2A specification, creating single-vendor dependency; (3) ACP complexity — MIME multipart parsing and structured message contracts increase implementation burden and bug surface; (4) ANP latency — DHT lookups and cryptographic verification introduce seconds of latency for initial discovery; (5) Cross-protocol semantic loss — translating between MCP tool calls and A2A tasks loses task structure information; (6) Protocol fragmentation — agents implementing only one protocol are siloed from the broader ecosystem; (7) Lies-in-the-Loop — none of the protocols prevent a compromised agent from sending deceptive messages to a human-in-the-loop; (8) Rapid evolution — all protocols are changing quickly (MCP had breaking changes between 2024 and 2025 versions), forcing agent implementations to chase protocol updates.
where_still_used: MCP: widely adopted — integrated into Claude Code, LangChain, LangGraph (v1.0 ships with MCP endpoint support), and 1000+ community-built MCP servers. A2A: deployed within Google's agent infrastructure; available to Google Cloud customers; adopted by select enterprise partners. ACP: contributed to Linux Foundation; reference implementation available; early adoption in enterprise messaging scenarios. ANP: open-source community with multiple implementations; testnets active; used in decentralized AI agent research projects. The survey paper (arXiv:2505.02279) documents adoption status across all four protocols. IEEE standardization working group launched late 2025. The protocol landscape is expected to consolidate through 2026-2027, with gateway-based interoperability as the likely near-term architecture.
